qnaData.stem:
Which of the following distinguish neuromyelitis optica (NMO) clinically from multiple sclerosis (MS)?
I. Attacks of optic neuritis can be bilateral and produce severe vision loss more commonly in NMO than in MS.
II. Brain MRIs are always normal in NMO.
III. Myelitis can be severe and transverse in NMO, but is rare in MS.
IV. NMO is more common in men, whereas MS is more common in women

A 64-year-old man presents with a urinary tract infection that has been unresponsive to a 5- day course of antibiotics started by his GP. Further questioning reveals he has suffered with problems with his chest for the last 10 years that he attributes to smoking since he was in his 20s. On examination he has a barrel-shaped chest; there is evidence of use of the accessory muscles of respiration and pursed lip breathing. Which of the following routine blood results is most likely to be found in this patient?

65-year-old obese woman underwent an open cholecystectomy for complicated gallstone disease 5 days ago. You are called to see her on the wards as she has become dyspneic, irritable and complaining of pain. Observations record a blood pressure of 110/60, pulse 66 and regular and saturations of 94% on 2L of oxygen. Her past medical history is of ischemic heart disease and hypertension treated with daily aspirin, atenolol, simvastatin and frusemide. Which of the following post-operative complications is the most likely diagnosis?

A 78-year-old female is evaluated by her primary care physician for worsening dyspnea on exertion. She has a history of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) treated with anti-tumor necrosis factor alpha therapy, which has controlled her joint manifestations. She had previously been on chronic prednisone for many years and has suffered several vertebral compression fractures with resulting kyphoscoliosis. On pulmonary function testing, she has an FEV1/FVC ratio of 82%, with an FVC of 58% predicted. Which of the following additional findings suggest that her dyspnea may be related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease?
options: A total lung capacity of 55% predicted with a diffusing capacity for carbon dioxide (DLCO) of 50% predicted
A total lung capacity of 55% predicted and a DLCO of 90% predicted
A total lung capacity of 90% predicted with a normal DLCO
A total lung capacity of 120% predicted with a DLCO of 60% predicted

Molecular medicine has made a lot of progress, metagenomics refers to study of:-
options: Study of environmental species potential to influence human biology
Study of microbiomes in relation to disease.
Study of metabolites in cells & organs and ways in their alteration in disease conditions
Study of library of proteins in cells or organs & its relation to complex relation to disease including posttranslational modification.

qnaData.stem: Evidence based medicine is best defined as
options: Research comparing results of approaching of treating disease to another one treating the same disease.
Clinical decision making based on expert opinion from clinical trial data
Clinical decision making based on data preferably by randomized controlled trials.
Critical methodological review & statistical analysis of summative data from existing clinical trials

Blood investigations show haemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, WBC 6.1x10^9/L, platelet count 180000 per mm^3, normal kidney function test and normal liver function test apart from an albumin value of 2.1 g/dL. options: Increase the dose of thyroxine to 75 micrograms daily
Reduce the dose of thyroxine to 25 micrograms daily

qnaData.stem: An 80-year old man on Glimeperide 2 mg twice daily and metformin 500 mg twice daily has been brought to the emergency in a comatose state. His son says that he has been forgetful lately and suspects that he might have taken double the dose of his usual medications. Capillary blood glucose is 38 mg/dL. Which of the following actions is most appropriate?
options: Give 50 mL of 50% dextrose mixed with 10 units of regular insulin intravenously
Check plasma glucose and give 50 mL of 50% dextrose intravenously if plasma glucose < 70 mg/dL

This morning she has been brought to the hospital with vomiting and drowsiness. Capillary blood glucose is reported as 'high'. Her arterial blood gas analysis is likely to show
options: Low pH, low HCO 3 - , low CO 2
Low pH, high HCO 3 - , high CO 2
Normal pH, high HCO 3 - , high CO 2
High pH, low HCO 3 - , low CO 2

qnaData.stem: A 22-year old BA student with a known history of asthma presents with acute shortness of breath. She has already been given three rounds of nebulisation with salbutamol and ipratropium and has also received intravenous magnesium sulfate. On examination, she is agitated and confused. Auscultation of chest reveals no added sounds and barely audible breath sounds. Arterial blood gas analysis shows a pH of 7.34, pO2 of 62 mm Hg and pCO2 of 51 mm Hg. What is the single most important next step?
options: Review by the ICU team with a view to intubate and ventilate the patient

qnaData.stem: A young lady on methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis wants to start her family planning. What is the most appropriate step?
options: Methotrexate should be replaced with leflunomide.
Methotrexate should be replaced with hydroxyl-chloroquine and sulfasalazine.
She should be advised against starting a family.

qnaData.stem: A 36 year old previously fit businessman who returned from a holiday in Thailand two months ago has presented with a five day history of loss of appetite, nausea, upper abdominal discomfort and yellowing of eyes and skin. On examination, he is jaundiced, has mild tender hepatomegaly but no flapping tremor. Blood tests reveal a bilirubin of 13 mg/ dL, ALT of 1456 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase of 396 U/L, normal prothrombin time and positive HBsAg and IgM against HBc. What should be the further management?
options: Reassure and monitor LFT with repeat HBsAg after six months
Start Tenofovir only if HBV DNA is more than 20,000 IU/ml
Start ursodeoxycholic acid and L-ornithine L-aspartate

qnaData.stem:
You are working as a Medical Officer at a District Hospital. A 60 year male smoker has presented to the emergency with a 3 hour history of central chest pain. He describes it as if "there was a bag of rice on my chest". There is no radiation. He has no known significant past medical history. On examination, BP is 160/92 and oxygen saturation is 92% on air. He looks anxious and sweaty. JVP is normal. There is no oedema. Heart sound is normal and chest is clear on auscultation. ECG is shown below:

What is the appropriate treatment?
options: Antiplatelets, nitrates and low-molecular weight heparin
Antiplatelets, nitrates and thrombolysis after ruling out contraindication
